coredata.dc:description: Hydrogel, a common carrier of photocatalyst that suffers from compromised catalytic efficiency, is still far from practical application. Herein, based on “computer chip-inspired design”, a novel nanocellulose/carbon dots hydrogel (NCH) was fabricated as superior intensifier instead of common carrier of sodium titanate nanofibre (STN), where carbon dots (CDs) enhanced amino group-induced adsorption for Cr(VI), promoted photocatalytic properties of STN via transferring the photogenerated electron-hole pairs and improved amino group-induced desorption for reduced product (Cr(III)) via electrostatic repulsion, showing an efficiency of 1 + 1 > 2. Adsorption and photocatalysis experiments demonstrated superior removal performance of the NCH incorporating STN, as shown by theoretical maximum adsorption capacity of 425.74 mg/g and kinetic constant of 0.0374 min−1 in the photocatalytic process, which was nearly 6.6 and 7.3 times of STN. A series of experiments was conducted to confirm the novel mechanism of CDs-enhanced adsorption–photocatalysis–desorption synergy. This work not only provides new insights into the fabrication of a superior intensifier for nanosized photocatalyst, but also proposes one new mechanism of CDs-enhanced adsorption–photocatalysis–desorption synergy, which is helpful for designing and optimizing nanosized photocatalyst.

head.ce:title: Computer chip-inspired design of nanocellulose/carbon dots hydrogel as superior intensifier of nano-sized photocatalyst for effective Cr(VI) removal

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrogel, a common carrier of photocatalyst that suffers from compromised catalytic efficiency, is still far from practical application. Herein, based on “computer chip-inspired design”, a novel nanocellulose/carbon dots hydrogel (NCH) was fabricated as superior intensifier instead of common carrier of sodium titanate nanofibre (STN), where carbon dots (CDs) enhanced amino group-induced adsorption for Cr(VI), promoted photocatalytic properties of STN via transferring the photogenerated electron-hole pairs and improved amino group-induced desorption for reduced product (Cr(III)) via electrostatic repulsion, showing an efficiency of 1 + 1 > 2. Adsorption and photocatalysis experiments demonstrated superior removal performance of the NCH incorporating STN, as shown by theoretical maximum adsorption capacity of 425.74 mg/g and kinetic constant of 0.0374 min−1 in the photocatalytic process, which was nearly 6.6 and 7.3 times of STN. A series of experiments was conducted to confirm the novel mechanism of CDs-enhanced adsorption–photocatalysis–desorption synergy. This work not only provides new insights into the fabrication of a superior intensifier for nanosized photocatalyst, but also proposes one new mechanism of CDs-enhanced adsorption–photocatalysis–desorption synergy, which is helpful for designing and optimizing nanosized photocatalyst.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cr(VI) pollution has become an increasingly serious problem in the world as a result of the rapid development of modern industry [1]. Various treatment methods have been introduced to remove Cr(VI) ions [2]. Photocatalysis is one of the most promising strategies, as it can convert highly toxic Cr(VI) to less harmful Cr(III) [3]. Recently, STN has been studied as an effective photocatalyst due to its high photosensitivity, stability, inexpensiveness and nontoxicity [4]. However, it can easily agglomerate, and separation is very difficult and requires complex equipment. Hence, developing a suitable carrier to overcome these drawbacks is a viable step toward the practical application.

Hydrogel is a promising carrier for photocatalyst due to its advantages of three-dimensional (3D) porous structure and high specific surface area. More recently, cellulose-based hydrogel has gained a significant attention due to its excellent adsorption ability toward Cr(VI) ion. Kim et al. fabricated a cationic surface-modified regenerated nanocellulose hydrogel and showed an excellent Cr(VI) removal capacity of 578 mg/g [5]. Pei et al. prepared a cellulose-based composite adsorbent via a facile crosslinking of polyethyleneimine/polyacrylamide/cellulose, and showed that the hydrogel had good regeneration performance [6]. Zhang et al. synthesized a novel cellulose hydrogel coating with a nanoscale Fe0 for Cr(VI) adsorption, and demonstrated that the network structure of the cellulose hydrogel served as a mass-transfer channel [7]. Hence, cellulose-based hydrogel shows a great potential as an effective carrier for photocatalyst. However, not enough studies were devoted to developing cellulose nanofibrils (CNs)-based hydrogel as a carrier of nanosized photocatalysts.

Although some progress was made by introducing the hydrogel as the carrier of nanosized photocatalysts, the compromised catalytic efficiency is still far from practical application [8]. Carbon dots (CDs) have piqued increasing interest due to their unique properties. CDs can be used to improve the adsorption and photocatalysis [9]. Yuan et al. prepared a fluorescent lignin-based hydrogel with cellulose nanofibers and CDs and then showed that CDs could promote the adsorption of Cr(VI) [10]. Wei et al. fabricated an Ag nanoparticle/CDs/TiO2 nanotube array composite and showed that the enhanced photocatalytic activity was due to the photoelectrical properties of CDs [11]. Hence, CDs can be promising candidates for the improvement of cellulose-based hydrogel.

Based on the above analysis, we proposed “computer chip-inspired design” and then fabricated a novel NCH using CNs, CDs and STN. In the NCH, CNs offered the “skeleton” of the hydrogel to construct a 3D porous structure increasing the specific surface area and providing additional active sites, CDs enhanced the amino group-induced adsorption for Cr(VI) and facilitated the transfer of the photogenerated electron from STN, and STN was a nanosized photocatalyst for generating the photogenerated electron. Various techniques were used to characterize the as-prepared NCH. A series of adsorption and photocatalysis experiments were used to characterize the removal performance of NCH. On this basis, one new mechanism of CDs-enhanced adsorption–photocatalysis–desorption synergy was proposed.
Materials and reagents were included in Supplementary material.

CNs were prepared according to previous studies with some modifications [12]. Bleached wood pulp is the starting material for preparing CNs. The bleaching of wood pulp is chemical processing of wood pulp to lighten its color. The bleaching procedures are as follows: wood pulp is exposed to chlorine, washed with a sodium hydroxide solution to remove lignin fragmented by chlorination, treated with sodium hypochlorite, washed with sodium hydroxide again, and given a final treatment with hypochlorite. The main component of the bleached wood pulp is cellulose; other components, such as lignin, pectin, and hemicelluloses, are almost removed. Here, bleached wood pulp (2 g), NaBr (0.2 g) and 2,2,6,6-Tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy (TEMPO) radical (0.032 g) were suspended in water (400 mL) and stirred while the NaClO powder (1.0 g) was added dropwise from a syringe and pH was adjusted at 10 with a NaOH solution (1 M). The reaction lasted for 5 h and was quenched using 100 mL ethanol. The oxidation products were washed to neutral and passed through an M110P microfluidiser five times. The resulting CNs suspension (0.5 wt %) was prepared with the carboxyl content of 0.68 mmol/g.

Citric acid (0.84 g) and ethylenediamine (1.1 mL) were dissolved in deionized water (20 mL). After stirring, the mixture was added to an autoclave and reacted at 200 °C for 5 h. A dark-brown solution was obtained and then dialyzed against deionized water. Finally, the concentration was set to 0.5 wt % by adding the appropriate amount of deionized water.

CDs were grafted onto CNs via N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC)/N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) coupling chemistry [13]. First, the CNs suspension (50 g, 0.5 wt %) was activated with 0.38 g EDC and 0.46 g NHS under vigorous stirring at pH 9.2 for 60 min. Second, a CDs solution (5 g, 0.5 wt %) was added. The reaction was left to proceed overnight under gentle mixing at room temperature. The resulting CNs–CDs suspension was washed and dialyzed against deionized water.

STN was synthesised using a solvothermal method [14]. An TiOSO4 powder (3.57 g) was added to NaOH solution (100 g, 40 wt %) with constant stirring. Then, the mixture was reacted in autoclave at 200 ℃ for 48 h. Precipitates were obtained and washed with deionized water. Next, the obtained STN powder was obtained using freeze-drying.

First, a specific amount of STN was dispersed into deionized water (4 mL). Acrylic acid (AA, 2.0 g) mixed well with a sodium hydroxide solution (4.72 mL, 5 mol/L) was then added by vigorous stirring. Next, CNs–CDs (1.8 g) and N′,N-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA, 0.035 g) were added and treated with ultrasonic for 30 min. Potassium persulfate (PPS, 0.03 g) was mixed by vigorous stirring for 1 min in a nitrogen atmosphere. The mixture was then maintained at 60 °C for 2 h. Afterwards, the as-prepared sample was immersed in deionized water for 2 days to remove unreacted impurities. Based on the STN amounts (0.01, 0.02, 0.05 and 0.10 g), the obtained samples were labelled NCH-1, NCH-2, NCH-3 and NCH-4, respectively. Under the same condition, a conventional hydrogel was synthesized using CNs (1.8 g) instead of CNs–CDs (1.8 g). The STN quality was set as 0.02 g, and this hydrogel was marked as NH-2.

Batch experiments were conducted in the dark to investigate Cr(VI) and Cr(III) adsorption. The prepared samples (0.6 g) were thoroughly mixed with the Cr(VI) or Cr(III) solution in 250-mL conical flasks. The conical flasks were then placed in a thermostatic shaker at a preset temperature and contact time. Adsorption isotherms were collected at a constant time (90 min) at various Cr(VI) or Cr(III) concentrations (from 10 to 50 mg/L). Furthermore, adsorption kinetics were investigated at different contact times (from 0 to 90 min) with a constant Cr(VI) or Cr(III) concentration (20 mg/L). In the Cr(VI) adsorption experiments, the Cr(VI) concentration was determined using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA) via 1,5-diphenylcarbazide method at 540 nm. In the Cr(III) adsorption experiments, the Cr(III) concentration was determined using an atomic absorption spectrophotometer (TAS-990, Beijing’s General Instrument, China). The sorption data were the average of three measurements.

The experiments were performed in a self-made reactor as mentioned in previous literatures [15]. A 500 W high pressure mercury lamp was used as the light source. The pH of the solutions was adjusted by adding negligible volume of 0.1 mol/L HNO3 or NaOH. First, the prepared sample was mixed thoroughly with Cr(VI) solutions (200 mL, 20 mg/L). The adsorption time in the dark was set as 90 min in which a 5 mL of reaction solution was collected and filtered every 15 min. The lamp was then switched on. The photocatalysis time was 105 min, in which a 5 mL Cr(VI) solution was collected and filtered every 15 min. Here, the total Cr concentrations were determined using an atomic absorption spectrophotometer (TAS-990, Beijing’s General Instrument, China). The Cr(III) concentration was then calculated from the difference between the total Cr and Cr(VI) concentration. Fig. S1 shows the photograph of the whole process.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JCM-7000, JEOL Ltd., Japan) was used to characterize the morphological feature. An energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrometer fitted to the scanning electron microscope on a nanoprobe mode was used for elemental analysis. The crystal structure of the samples were determined by X-ray diffractometry (XRD) using a Rigaku D/MAX2500 diffractometer (Rigaku Corporation, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded using an IN 10™ spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) with attenuated total reflection (ATR) method. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded on an F-7000 spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) were measured via a UV-2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Elemental analysis was performed by using Sigma Probe X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (VG Scientific, UK). N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm was recorded by a Micromeritics Tri-Star II surface area and porosity analyzer (Micromeritics Instrument Co., Ltd., USA). Time-resolved PL spectra were measured by an FL3–111 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horida, Japan). Supporting material presents the detailed electrochemical measurement experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the computer chip-inspired intensifier
ce:section.ce:para: Instead of a common carrier, the design of the intensifier for nanosized photocatalyst is inspired from a computer chip, which is a collection device equipped with one main processor and one assist processor. These two processors are used to process data, and the collection device is used to collect data. As a result, the whole computer chip has grater effectiveness. Here, NH, STN and CDs were similar to the collection device, the main processor and the assist processor, respectively. When these three parts were made into NCH, it could have an effective ability to remove Cr(VI). Fig. 1 shows a schematic illustration of NCH synthesis. First, CNs were prepared by TEMPO-mediated oxidation of bleached wood pulp, followed by high-pressure homogenization. Meanwhile, amino-modified CDs were synthesized through hydrothermal reactions. Second, CNs were connected with CDs to form CNs–CDs by condensation reactions. Third, the nanosized photocatalyst STN, the skeletal material CNs–CDs, the monomer AA and the crosslinker MBA were mixed well. Finally, the initiator PPS was added, and the 3D porous structured NCH was synthesized via free-radical polymerization.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was used to observe the morphology of the prepared samples. As shown in Fig. 2a, CNs exhibited a smooth and uniform surface. CDs were spherical with an average diameter size of 3.0 nm (Fig. S2). Furthermore, rodlike CNs–CDs had a coarse surface (Fig. 2b), confirming the successful attachment of CDs to CNs [16]. Meanwhile, the rodlike STN was characterized (Fig. S3). As displayed in Fig. 2c, the SEM image of NCH-2 shows the successful construction of a 3D porous structure. The existence of STN in NCH-2 was confirmed by EDS spectrum (Fig. S4). N2 adsorption–desorption test was used to illustrate the surface areas of the prepared samples. Fig. S5 shows N2 adsorption–desorption curves of CNs, CNs–CDs and NCH-2. The results of the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface (hereafter SBET) were summarized. The SBET of CNs–CDs (7.023 m2/g) was slightly larger than that of CNs (6.696 m2/g), which was attributed to the loading of CDs into CNs. Most importantly, NCH-2 had the largest SBET (21.80 m2/g) among the three samples, which was attributed to the construction of 3D porous structure [17].

Meanwhile, XRD was performed to investigate the crystallographic characteristics of CNs, CNs–CDs, STN and NCH-2 (Fig. 2d). CNs exhibited three peaks at 14.9° ((1
1
¯
0) plane), 16.2° ((110) plane) and 22.7° ((200) plane), respectively [18]. The CNs–CDs had XRD patterns similar to CNs, suggesting that most of the original crystal structure of CNs was preserved [19]. Meanwhile, sodium STN exhibited eight diffraction peaks at 10.4° ((100) plane), 24.9° ((201) plane), 29.6° ((003) plane), 34.4° ((002) plane), 35.9° ((301) plane), 38.8° ((013) plane), 48.4° ((020) plane) and 52.8° ((411) plane), respectively [20]. Notably, the XRD patterns of NCH-2 contained diffraction peaks of STN and CNs–CDs. This phenomenon could be attributed to the loading of STN and CNs–CDs into NCH-2.

Fig. 2e shows the FTIR spectra of CNs, CNs–CDs, STN and NCH-2. Both CNs and CNs–CDs exhibited one strong peak at 1053 cm−1, corresponding to the C–O stretching vibration mode [21]. Meanwhile, one new band at 1650 cm−1 could be contributed from the –CO–NH– stretching mode, meaning that CDs were crosslinked with CNs [22]. The typical spectrum of STN revealed one typical peak at 910 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of Ti–O [23]. For NCH-2, the abovementioned characteristic bands for STN and CNs–CDs were weakened.

XPS was performed to study the chemical binding states of the samples. The NCH-2 sample contained C, N, Ti and O elements (Fig. 2f). The curve fitting of the O1s XPS spectra in Fig. 2g exhibits two peaks at 531.18 and 532.98 eV, which were attributed to C–O and CO, respectively [24]. Meanwhile, the N1s spectrum of the NCH-2 was deconvoluted into three peaks (399.18, 399.86 and 402.70 eV), which were assigned to –N = , –NH2 and –NH3 +, respectively (Fig. 2h) [25]. The Ti2p XPS spectrum reveals two broad peaks centred at 458.28 and 463.18 eV, corresponding to Ti2p3/2 and Ti2p1/2, respectively (Fig. 2i), this energy confirmed the presence of Ti4+ [26]. More importantly, the XPS spectra confirmed the existence of CDs (containing N element) and STN (containing Ti element) in the NCH-2.

The pH of the Cr(VI) solution is a crucial parameter in the adsorption process. As shown in Fig. S6, the adsorption capacity (Q e) first increased and then decreased, with the highest adsorption capacity was obtained at pH = 2. This change was closely related to the ionic states of NCH-2 and Cr(VI) [27]. The amino group was deprotonated in this acidic pH range. Meanwhile, at pH 1, uncharged H2CrO4 was the main species, resulting in lower Q e. At pH 2–6, HCrO4 − and Cr2O7 2− became the major species of Cr(VI) ions, and then electrostatic attraction between Cr(VI) ions and NCH-2 resulted in high Q e. At higher pH (>2), Q e was further reduced due to deprotonation [28]. This result implied that the optimum pH for Cr(VI) sorption by NCH-2 was 2.

The adsorption isotherms of Cr(VI) on NCH-2 and NH-2 are shown in Fig. 3a. At the same concentration, the Q e of NCH-2 was greater than that of NH-2, confirming that the addition of CDs favored Cr(VI) adsorption. Additionally, the sorption isotherm of NCH-2 was fitted using the Langmuir (Eq. (1)) and Freundlich (Eq. (2)) models, separately [29].

where Q e and Q max represent the equilibrium sorption capacity and the theoretical maximum sorption capacity, respectively, C e represents the equilibrium concentration, K L represents a Langmuir binding constant, K F and n represent the Freundlich constants and R 2 denotes the correlation coefficient [30]. Fig. 3b and c illustrate the fitting results, and Table S1 lists the corresponding parameters. According to R 2, the Langmuir isotherm model was more suitable for the obtained data than the Freundlich model, confirming the monolayer adsorption process of Cr(VI) on NCH-2. The Q max of NCH-2 from the Langmuir model was 425.74 mg/g, which was about 6.6 times as high as that of STN (64.35 mg/g, as shown in Fig. S7).

The contact time is another important factor for evaluating adsorption performance. As shown in Fig. 3d, when the contact time increased, the adsorption capacity (Q t) also increased. In contrast, at the same contact time, the Q t of NCH-2 was greater than that of NH-2, confirming that CDs in NCH-2 could accelerate the Cr(VI) adsorption. Moreover, pseudo-first-order (Eq. (3)) and pseudo-second-order (Eq. (4)) kinetic models were used to fit the kinetic data of NCH-2.

where Q t and Q e represent the amounts of Cr(VI) adsorbed by the sample at time t and at equilibrium, respectively, and k 1 and k 2 represent the corresponding adsorption rate constants. Fig. 3e and f illustrate the fitting results, and Table S2 lists the corresponding parameters. The R 2 value from the pseudo-second-order model was higher than that from the pseudo-first-order model, confirming that chemisorption was the rate-controlling step [31].

The adsorption measurements of Cr(III) were performed on NH-2 and NCH-2 to clarify the Cr(VI) removal mechanism. Fig. S8 shows Q e for Cr(III) of NH-2 and NCH-2 as a function of Cr(III) concentration. At the same concentration, Q e of NCH-2 was lower than that of NH-2. The Langmuir model was used to fit the isotherm experimental data. Table S3 lists the fitting results. It was shown the maximum adsorption capacity for Cr(III) of NCH-2 was lower than that of NH-2. Fig. S9 shows the Q t for Cr(III) of NH-2 and NCH-2 as a function of contact time. The Q t of NCH-2 was lower than that of NH-2, indicating that Cr(III) sorption was unfavored by CDs. Moreover, the pseudo-second-order model was used to fit these kinetic data, and Table S4 lists the results. It was shown that the adsorption rate for Cr(III) of NCH-2 was lower than that of NH-2. These comparisons confirmed that CDs prohibited the adsorption of Cr(III), thereby facilitating Cr(III) desorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synergetic effects of adsorption and photocatalysis on Cr(VI) removal
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption and photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) by STN, NH-2 and NCH-2 were observed ( Fig. 4a). After 90 min of adsorption in the dark, an equilibrium state was reached. Then, the photocatalytic reduction reaction proceeded. After 105 min, the Cr(VI) concentrations treated with STN, CH-2 and NCH-2 were 11.72, 5.62 and 0.12 mg/L, and the corresponding removal efficiencies were 41.4 %, 71.9 % and 99.4 %, respectively. The NCH-2 sample showed the best removal efficiency. These experimental data of photocatalytic reduction were fitted by the pseudo-first-order model (Fig. 4b). The photocatalytic reduction rate constant (k) of STN, NH-2 and NCH-2 was 0.0051, 0.0120 and 0.0374 min−1, respectively. The k of NCH-2 was approximately 7.3 times that of STN. The result indicated that the NCH-2 had better Cr(VI) removal performance compared with the two other samples due to CDs-enhanced adsorption–photocatalysis–desorption synergy.

Fig. 4c shows the effects of pH on Cr(VI) removal. In the pH range of 2–5, NCH-2 exhibited the highest removal efficiency at pH 2. The experimental data of photocatalytic reduction were studied using the pseudo-first-order kinetic model (Fig. 4d). The k values at the different pH (from 2 to 5) were 0.0374, 0.0175, 0.0121 and 0.0081 min−1, respectively. These differences were attributed to two factors: firstly, HCrO4 − and Cr2O7 2− are predominating species at the observed pH range. The increase in the pH value reduced protonation, which then reduced Cr(VI) adsorption ability. Secondly, the decrease in pH value decreased the amount of H+, which negatively affected structural changes from Cr(VI) to Cr(III) [32].

The effect of STN loading amount on Cr(VI) removal was investigated (Fig. 4e). An increase in the loading amount of STN from 0.01 to 0.10 g, improved Cr(VI) removal efficiency. The Cr(VI) removal efficiency of NCH-2 with 0.02 g STN reached 100 % within 105 min. When the loading amount was increased to 0.05, the majority of the 3D porous structure in NCH was blocked (as shown in Fig. S10), limiting the adsorptive and photocatalytic activity [33]. Additionally, the experimental data of photocatalytic reduction were studied using the pseudo-first-order model (Fig. 4f). The k with different STN loading amounts in NCH-1, NCH-2, NCH-3 and NCH-4 were 0.0105, 0.0374, 0.0180 and 0.0144 min−1, respectively. The result showed that NCH-2 had an optimal loading amount of STN.

The effect of initial concentration on Cr(VI) removal was investigated (Fig. 4g). When the initial Cr(VI) concentration was 10 mg/L, the removal efficiency of NCH-2 was approximately 100 % in 105 min. Meanwhile, when the initial Cr(VI) concentration was 40 mg/L, the removal efficiency of NCH-2 was 69.9 % in 105 min. Thus, an increase in the Cr(VI) initial concentration degrades Cr(VI) removal efficiency. Additionally, the experimental data of photocatalytic reduction were studied using the pseudo-first-order kinetic model (Fig. 4h). The k at four different initial concentrations of 10, 20, 30 and 40 mg/L was 0.0479, 0.0374, 0.0182 and 0.0123 min−1, respectively. These changes were attributed to the saturation of active sites at high concentrations [34].

Reusability is an important factor to verify the stability and recyclability of materials [35]. The used NCH-2 (0.6 g) was immersed into a sodium hydroxide solution (100 mL, 0.1 M) for 3 h. The NCH-2 sample was then washed three times with deionized water until neutral condition. Five consecutive cycles using the condition as described in Section 2.8 were accomplished and the removal efficiency of Cr(VI) ions after each cycle was measured. As shown in Fig. 5a, the removal ability of NCH-2 was kept above 85 % of its initial value after five cycles. Additionally, the structural integrity of NCH-2 was evidenced by XRD analyses. The crystal structure of NCH-2 was well preserved after five cycles of adsorption–desorption (Fig. 5b) [36]. These results confirmed that NCH-2 obtained good stability and durability after multiple recycling experiments.

Fig. 6a displays the FTIR spectra of NCH-2 before and after Cr(VI) adsorption. The peak at 3339 cm−1 was attributed to the O–H and N–H stretching modes [37], and the peak at 1560 cm−1 was attributed to the carboxylate ion [38]. After sorption, these two peaks decreased, and one peak at 902 cm−1 resulting from Cr emerged. The results confirmed that the amino and carboxyl groups played a key role in Cr(VI) sorption [39].

XPS was employed to investigate the adsorption process. Fig. 6b shows the survey scan of NCH-2. After Cr(VI) adsorption, one new peak at 579.8 eV resulting from Cr emerged, indicating that Cr was transferred onto NCH-2. Fig. 6c shows the Cr2p spectrum of NCH-2 after Cr(VI) adsorption. The peaks of Cr2p1/2 and Cr2p3/2 were found at 588 and 579 eV, respectively. Additionally, the peak at 588 eV was fitted by two-component peaks at 588.88 eV (Cr(VI)) and 586.38 eV (Cr(III)), respectively. Another peak at 579 eV was also fitted by two-component peaks at 579.78 eV (Cr(VI)) and 576.68 eV (Cr(III)), respectively [40]. These changes confirmed the Cr(VI) reduction. When part of the Cr(VI) ion was reduced to Cr(III), the hydroxyl group of NCH-2 was oxidized, which followed the main mechanism of Cr(VI) removal by natural biomaterials, i.e., “adsorption-coupled reduction”. The curve fitting of the O1s XPS spectrum before Cr(VI) adsorption in Fig. 6d showed two peaks at 532.98 and 531.18 eV, which could be attributed to CO and C–O, respectively. After adsorption, these peaks moved to lower binding energies, indicating ionic bonding between –COO− and Cr(III) [41]. Fig. 6e shows N1s XPS spectra of NCH-2. Before adsorption, there were three peaks at 399.18, 399.86 and 402.70 eV, which were assigned to –N = , –NH2 and –NH3 +, respectively. After adsorption, no new peak was observed, demonstrating that the interaction was not complexation but an electrostatic attraction between the protonated amino group and Cr(VI). The Ti2p spectrum of NCH-2 (Fig. 6f) shows only two peaks at 463.18 and 458.28 eV. After Cr(VI) sorption, no new peak was observed. This result indicated that the reduced Cr (III) would reach through ion exchange with STN.
UV–vis DRS spectra, valence band (VB) XPS spectra, time-resolved PL spectra and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were used to examine the photocatalytic reduction mechanism.

UV–vis DRS were performed to evaluate the optical properties of STN powder, NH-2 and NCH-2 ( Fig. 7a). It was found that these three samples exhibited good UV light responses. Remarkably, the introduction of CDs promoted the UV light absorption ability of the NCH-2 [42]. The band gap energy (E g) was computed using the Kubelka–Munk function (Fig. 7a’s inset figure). The NCH-2 sample had the smallest E g value of 3.68 eV, indicating a good photoabsorption performance [43].

From VB XPS valence spectra (Fig. 7b), the VB potentials of STN powder, NH-2 and NCH-2 were 3.03, 2.93 and 2.88 eV, respectively. Based on the E g and VB potential, the conduction band (CB) potentials of the three samples were −0.75, −0.78 and −0.80 eV, respectively.

The kinetics of charge carriers was investigated using time-resolved PL spectra (Fig. 7c). Both NCH-2 and NH-2 exhibited double exponential decay characteristics. The average lifetimes of NCH-2 and NH-2 were estimated to be 1.09 and 1.12 ns, respectively. The comparison of these two calculated values confirmed that the loading of CDs into NCH-2 could enhance the separation of charge carriers [44].

Additionally, the recombination efficiency of the photogenerated electron–hole pairs was further analysed using PL spectra. As shown in Fig. 7d, the three samples exhibited the same peak at around 480 nm, confirming the loading of STN. The intensity of NCH-2′s peak was the lowest, indicating the lowest recombination efficiency of photogenerated electron–hole pairs. These changes may be due to the loading of CDs. As the electronic conductor, CDs could reduce the diffusion distance of the carriers to the surface, and then accelerate the transportation of the carriers and inhibit the recombination of electrons and holes [45].

Fig. 7e illustrates the photocurrent responses of STN powder, NH-2 and NCH-2. Among these materials, NCH-2 had the largest saturated photocurrent density, suggesting that the construction of the 3D porous structure and the introduction of CDs were favourable to the separation and movement of photogenerated charges [46]. These results were consistent with the conclusion obtained from PL spectra.

Meanwhile, EIS was used to explain the electron-transfer efficiency. Fig. 7f shows the semicircular Nyquist plots. The NCH-2 sample had the smallest arc radius, corresponding to an improved charge transfer rate [47], which was consistent with the photocurrent data.

To interpret the photocatalytic activities, we examined the effects of four free-radical trapping agents, including ethanol, isopropanol (IPA), benzoquinone (BQ) and AgNO3, which acted as the holes (h+), hydroxyl radicals (·OH), superoxide anion (·O2 −) and electron (e−) scavengers [48]. The obtained results are illustrated in Fig. 8a. With the addition of ethanol, IPA, BQ, AgNO3 and no scavenger, k was 0.09049, 0.03255, 0.01809, 0.01553 and 0.0374 min−1, respectively (Fig. 8b). Hence, e− and·O2 − were the main reactive species in the photocatalytic reduction. Furthermore, EPR spin-trapping using 5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) measurements was performed to confirm the functions of·O2 − radicals [49]. DMPO-·O2− signals were observed under UV irradiation, and the subsequent peak intensities were stable (Fig. 8c). In the case of NCH-2, the loading of CDs helped produce more e− and·O2 − because CDs could act as electron acceptors [50]. This may be one reason why CDs could enhance the photocatalytic activity of NCH-2.

To elucidate the removal mechanism, we studied the changes of Cr species during the process of adsorption and photocatalysis with STN, NH-2 and NCH-2 ( Fig. 9). In the process of adsorption, the formation of Cr(III) ions was found in the solutions soaking NH-2 and NCH-2, while the formation of Cr(III) ions was not found in the solution soaking STN. The result indicated that there was “adsorption-coupled reduction” in the NH-2 and NCH-2, which was mainly attributed to the existence of CNs. Meanwhile, the Cr(III) concentration in the solution soaking NCH-2 was higher than that in the solution soaking NH-2, mainly because CDs facilitated the release of Cr(III) into the solution. In the process of photocatalysis, Cr(III) concentrations in these three solutions increased with UV irradiation time, conforming that some Cr(VI) was reduced to Cr(III). Moreover, both the decrease in Cr(VI) concentration and the increase in Cr(III) concentration followed the same order, i.e., NCH-2 > NH-2 > STN. These changes were mainly due to the photoreduction of Cr(VI), thereby indicating that the NCH-2 had the best photocatalytic ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of CDs-enhanced adsorption-photocatalysis-desorption synergy
ce:section.ce:para:
On the basis of the above results, the mechanism of CDs-enhanced adsorption–photocatalysis–desorption synergy for removing Cr(VI) by NCH was proposed ( Fig. 10). First, negatively charged Cr(VI) ions could be absorbed via electrostatic force due to the introduction of amino-modified CDs into NCH [51]. Under UV irradiation, photogenerated electrons in STN were excited [52]. With the help of CDs, most of the photogenerated electrons were quickly transferred from STN to the surface of CDs. Cr(VI) ions adsorbed on CDs were then reduced to Cr(III) ions by electronic reduction. At the same time, some hydroxyl groups of CNs were oxidized. Next, Cr(III) ions departed from the surface of CDs due to the electrostatic repulsion between amino-modified CDs and Cr(III) ions, thereby freeing many active sites for the next process of adsorption and photocatalysis. The abovementioned CDs-enhanced synergistic mechanism involved CDs enhanced amino group-induced adsorption for Cr(VI), promoted photocatalytic properties of STN via transferring the photogenerated electron and improved amino group-induced desorption for reduced product (Cr(III)) via electrostatic repulsion, showing an efficiency of 1 + 1 > 2. Moreover, the Cr(VI) reduction was described by using the following equations [53,54].

In this process, the novel NCH was more like a collection device equipped with two processors, showing the efficiency of 1 + 1 > 2 reaction.

A novel NCH was fabricated as a superior intensifier instead of common carrier of STN based on “computer chip-inspired design” and then applied to remove Cr(VI) with adsorption–photocatalysis–desorption synergy. The physical and chemical properties of NCH were studied. Moreover, the construction of 3D porous structure and the loading of amino-modified CDs were confirmed. A series of adsorption and photocatalysis experiments showed the superior removal performance of NCH-2, as demonstrated by the large adsorption capacity of 425.74 mg/g and high kinetic constant of 0.0374 min−1 in the photocatalytic process, which was nearly 6.6 times and 7.3 times of STN. This superior performance was due to the mechanism of CDs-enhanced adsorption–photocatalysis–desorption synergy. This work not only provided a promising strategy towards preparing superior intensifier of nano-sized photocatalyst but also proposed one new mechanism of CDs-enhanced adsorption–photocatalysis–desorption which was applicable for the improvement of nanosized photocatalyst.

Xinmiao Qi: Investigation. Junwen Peng: Software. Xuefeng Zhang: Investigation. Haoxuan Cai: Investigation. Yong Huang: Software. Jianzheng Qiao: Software. Yucong Guo: Validation. Xin Guo: Conceptualization, Writing - Original Draft, Supervision. Yiqiang Wu: Supervision.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 31890771), Changsha Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. kq2208414), Hunan Youth Science and Technology Innovation Talent Project (Grant No. 2020RC3043) and Training Plan of Young Backbone Teachers in Colleges and Universities of Hunan Province, China (Grant No. 71).

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogel, a common carrier of photocatalyst that suffers from compromised catalytic efficiency, is still far from practical application. Based on ‘computer chip-inspired design’, a nanocellulose/carbon dots hydrogel is fabricated as superior intensifier instead of common carrier of sodium titanate nanofibre, where carbon dots (CDs) enhance amino group-induced adsorption for Cr(VI), promote photocatalytic properties via transferring the photogenerated electron-hole pairs and improve amino group-induced desorption for reduced product (Cr(III)) via electrostatic repulsion, showing an efficiency of 1 + 1 > 2. This work proposes one new mechanism of CDs-enhanced adsorption–photocatalysis–desorption synergy which is helpful for optimizing nano-sized photocatalyst.
